summary: DHH reports that 37signals achieved exceptional uptime across all major applications in 2023, the same year they moved seven apps out of the cloud. HEY email hit 99.99% uptime despite the migration, and Basecamp 2 achieved 100% uptime for the second consecutive year. He attributes this success to choosing boring, mature technologies and resisting unnecessary complexity. The post serves as evidence for his ongoing argument that most companies don't need cloud infrastructure to achieve reliability.
key_points: All seven major 37signals applications achieved at least 99.99% uptime in 2023 despite migrating off the cloud
Basecamp 2 hit 100% uptime for two consecutive years running on owned hardware
Their stack is deliberately boring: F5s, Linux, KVM, Docker, MySQL, Redis, Elasticsearch, and Rails
Programmers' attraction to complexity is the real enemy of reliability, not self-hosted infrastructure
Only true hyperscalers like Netflix or Google face problems that justify cloud-scale complexity
money_quotes: So hitting the hallowed four nines in a year when everything had to change about how we run HEY is a major accomplishment that makes me proud.
To the question about whether you can ensure reliability and stability outside the cloud, no application makes this point clearer for us than Basecamp 2.
But programmers are attracted to complexity like moths to a flame. The more convoluted the systems diagram, the greater the intellectual masturbation.
Our commitment to resisting that is the key ingredient in this uptime success.
There's nothing fancy about our stack, and very little complexity either. We don't need people with PhDs in Kubernetes or specialists in exotic data stores.
But for the other 99.99% of us? It's a siren song to model our infrastructure in their image.
You don't need the cloud to get good uptimes. You need mature technologies run on redundant hardware with good backups. Same as it ever was.
key_insight: Exceptional uptime comes not from cloud infrastructure or complex systems, but from choosing boring technologies and resisting the programmer instinct toward unnecessary complexity.
